agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"DynaPricing is a real-time dynamic pricing system for delivery services that adjusts prices based on demand levels. The CEO describes it at a high level with clear intent (demand-based pricing, automation, guardrails) but leaves most specifics undefined. This is a pre-spec, vision-stage description.","bounded_context":"Pricing","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"DynaPricing","definition":"The dynamic pricing product/system that adjusts delivery prices in real time based on demand"},{"term":"Demand","definition":"A measure of delivery request volume relative to capacity in a given area/time window — exact definition unspecified"},{"term":"Guardrails","definition":"Upper and/or lower bounds on price adjustments to prevent extreme pricing outcomes"},{"term":"Base Price","definition":"Implied but unstated — the reference price from which adjustments are made"},{"term":"Price Adjustment","definition":"The delta applied to a delivery price in response to demand signals, either upward or downward"}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"Delivery prices must increase when demand is high","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is a business policy — the direction of the correlation (high demand → higher price) is a strategic choice, not a physical or data-integrity constraint. A VP could decide to freeze prices during high demand for a promotion.","testable_assertion":"Given demand classified as 'high', the price adjustment factor must be > 1.0 (or the additive adjustment > 0) relative to the base price"},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"Delivery prices must decrease when demand is low","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"Same reasoning as BR-1 — lowering prices in low demand is a competitive strategy, not a system integrity constraint.","testable_assertion":"Given demand classified as 'low', the price adjustment factor must be < 1.0 (or the additive adjustment < 0) relative to the base price"},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"Price adjustments must occur in real time, not batch or delayed","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"'Real time' is a performance/freshness policy. The system doesn't become corrupt if pricing runs on a 5-minute delay — it just becomes less effective. A VP could choose near-real-time for cost reasons.","testable_assertion":"The time between a demand signal change and the corresponding price update being available to customers must be below a defined latency threshold (threshold TBD)"},{"id":"BR-4","rule":"Price adjustments must be fully automated with no manual intervention required in the normal flow","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"Automation is an operational policy. Manual overrides or human-in-the-loop could be added without corrupting system state.","testable_assertion":"Given a change in demand, the system must compute and apply a new price without requiring human approval"},{"id":"BR-5","rule":"Price guardrails must exist to prevent prices from exceeding acceptable bounds","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"This is a state integrity constraint. A price that goes 'crazy' (e.g., $0.00 or $10,000 for a local delivery) represents corrupt output. Without guardrails, the system can produce nonsensical prices that violate basic business viability and customer trust. The guardrail mechanism itself is an invariant — the specific thresholds are policy.","testable_assertion":"No computed delivery price may be published to customers that falls outside the configured [minimum_price, maximum_price] bounds"},{"id":"BR-6","rule":"The final delivery price must never be zero or negative","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"A zero or negative delivery price means the company pays the customer to deliver — this is a data integrity violation. No business scenario makes this valid for a for-profit courier.","testable_assertion":"For any demand level, the final customer-facing delivery price must be > 0"},{"id":"BR-7","rule":"Pricing must balance competitiveness and profitability — prices should not be so high they destroy demand, nor so low they destroy margin","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is a strategic business objective, not a hard constraint. The exact balance is a tunable policy decision.","testable_assertion":"The pricing algorithm's output, when applied across historical demand data, must produce prices that fall within a target margin range (range TBD) while maintaining order volume above a minimum threshold (threshold TBD)"},{"id":"BR-8","rule":"Price adjustments must be driven by demand signals, not arbitrary or random","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"If the system produces price changes that are not traceable to a demand input, the system is behaving incorrectly — this is a correctness invariant. Every price change must have a causal demand signal.","testable_assertion":"Every price adjustment event must reference the demand signal(s) that triggered it; no price change may occur absent a demand input"}],"acceptance_criteria":[],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"Demand signal is unavailable or stale (e.g., data feed outage)","expected_behavior":"Undefined by CEO. System must have a fallback — likely revert to base price or last known good price. MUST NOT produce $0 or unbounded prices."},{"scenario":"Demand spikes to extreme levels (e.g., 10x normal) during an emergency or event","expected_behavior":"Guardrails cap the price. But CEO hasn't addressed surge pricing ethics, regulatory caps, or PR risk."},{"scenario":"Demand drops to zero (no orders in a region/time window)","expected_behavior":"Price floors prevent the price from going below cost. But minimum price is undefined."},{"scenario":"Two simultaneous demand signals conflict (e.g., regional high demand vs. city-wide low demand)","expected_behavior":"Undefined. Granularity of demand measurement is not specified."},{"scenario":"Customer sees one price, but demand changes before order confirmation","expected_behavior":"Race condition — should the quoted price be honored or re-calculated? Not addressed."},{"scenario":"Competitor drops prices dramatically","expected_behavior":"CEO mentions 'competitive' but no rule about competitor price tracking or response. Unclear if DynaPricing considers external signals."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"Dynamic pricing can significantly increase revenue during peak demand and maintain volume during troughs. However, poorly calibrated guardrails risk either leaving money on the table or destroying demand through overpricing.","operational":"Full automation reduces manual pricing work but introduces complexity in monitoring, alerting, and incident response when the algorithm misbehaves. Real-time requirement implies infrastructure investment.","user":"Customers may perceive dynamic pricing as unfair ('surge pricing backlash'). Transparency of pricing rationale is not addressed but is critical to trust and retention.","risk":"HIGH — underspecified guardrails, no mention of regulatory compliance (price gouging laws vary by jurisdiction), no mention of price transparency requirements, no fallback strategy defined. Reputational risk from surge pricing perception.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Customers","impact":"Prices will fluctuate — some pay more, some pay less. Perception of fairness is a major concern. No communication strategy defined."},{"group":"Drivers/Couriers","impact":"Not mentioned at all. Dynamic pricing may affect driver earnings, incentives, and supply-side behavior. Critical gap."},{"group":"Finance/Revenue Team","impact":"Needs margin guardrails, reporting on pricing effectiveness, and audit trails for price decisions."},{"group":"Operations/Support","impact":"Will handle complaints about price fluctuations. Need visibility into why a specific price was charged."},{"group":"Legal/Compliance","impact":"Price gouging laws, consumer protection regulations, and potential audit requirements are entirely unaddressed."},{"group":"Product/Engineering","impact":"Must build real-time demand sensing, pricing engine, guardrail configuration, monitoring, and fallback systems."}]},"confidence":{"level":30,"reasoning":"This is a CEO vision statement, not a specification. It contains exactly one concrete rule (demand up → price up, demand down → price down) and two vague directives ('competitive but profitable', 'some guardrails'). Almost every critical detail is missing: demand measurement methodology, guardrail thresholds, pricing algorithm, granularity (per-zone? per-route?), latency SLA, fallback behavior, price-lock/quote behavior, driver-side impact, regulatory compliance, and audit requirements. The word 'probably' before guardrails further weakens confidence. Acceptance criteria cannot be responsibly defined from this description alone.","high_confidence_areas":["Prices go up when demand is high (explicitly stated)","Prices go down when demand is low (explicitly stated)","System will be automated (explicitly stated)","Some form of guardrails will exist (stated, though hedged with 'probably')"],"low_confidence_areas":["What constitutes 'high' vs 'low' demand — no thresholds or methodology","Guardrail specifics — min/max bounds, who configures them, how they're enforced","Definition of 'real time' — sub-second? minutes?","Granularity of pricing — per zone, per route, per time window?","Fallback behavior when demand signals are unavailable","Whether quoted prices are locked or can change before confirmation","Competitive positioning mechanism — is it just demand-based or also competitor-aware?","Driver/courier side effects and incentive alignment","Regulatory and legal compliance requirements"],"assumptions":["A 'base price' exists from which adjustments are made — CEO didn't mention this but it's implied","Demand is measurable and quantifiable in near-real-time","Guardrails include both a floor and a ceiling","The system serves a courier/delivery business with per-delivery pricing","Price can never be zero or negative — basic business viability assumption"]},"escalations":[{"type":"ambiguity","description":"'Demand' is undefined. Is it order volume per hour? Orders per driver? Zone-level or city-level? The entire pricing model depends on how demand is measured, and the CEO provided no definition.","affected_stakeholders":["Product","Engineering","Finance"],"options":["Define demand as order volume relative to available driver capacity in a geographic zone","Define demand as raw order count per time window","Define demand as a composite signal including volume, capacity, and historical patterns"],"recommendation":"Escalate to product leadership to define demand measurement methodology before any design work begins."},{"type":"ambiguity","description":"'Guardrails so prices don't go crazy' — no specifics on bounds, who sets them, or how they're enforced. The CEO hedged with 'probably', suggesting this isn't fully decided.","affected_stakeholders":["Customers","Legal","Finance","Operations"],"options":["Fixed min/max price bounds configurable by ops","Percentage-based caps relative to base price (e.g., never more than 3x base)","Jurisdiction-aware caps that comply with local price gouging regulations"],"recommendation":"Define guardrail types and ownership immediately. Legal must review before launch due to price gouging law risk."},{"type":"missing_context","description":"No mention of price-lock behavior. When a customer is quoted a price, can it change before they confirm the order? This is a critical UX and trust issue.","affected_stakeholders":["Customers","Product","Engineering"],"options":["Lock price for a time window (e.g., 5 minutes) after quote","Re-price at confirmation time with customer notification","Lock price at quote time unconditionally"],"recommendation":"Define price-lock policy before implementation. Customers will file complaints if prices change between viewing and ordering."},{"type":"missing_context","description":"Driver/courier impact is completely absent. Dynamic pricing affects supply-side economics — higher prices may need to translate to higher driver payouts to attract supply during peak demand.","affected_stakeholders":["Drivers/Couriers","Operations","Finance"],"options":["DynaPricing adjusts customer price only, driver pay unchanged","DynaPricing adjusts both customer price and driver incentives proportionally","Driver pay is a separate system that reacts to DynaPricing signals"],"recommendation":"Escalate to product leadership. Supply-side incentive alignment is critical to making demand-based pricing work — higher prices without more drivers just means unfulfilled orders."},{"type":"missing_context","description":"No regulatory or legal compliance requirements mentioned. Many jurisdictions have price gouging laws, especially during emergencies. Dynamic pricing systems in delivery have faced regulatory scrutiny.","affected_stakeholders":["Legal","Compliance","Executive Leadership"],"options":["Build jurisdiction-aware pricing caps from day one","Launch without regulatory caps and add later","Consult legal before any implementation"],"recommendation":"Legal review is mandatory before implementation begins. This is a compliance risk that could result in fines or forced rollback."}],"rejection_reasons":[]}
